phase-plan.purpose: Make the public site and customer portal operational by turning submissions, scheduling, support, estimates, invoices, and recurring-plan scheduling into first-party Payload relationships, with Resend and Stripe reserved for delivery and payment rails rather than core CRM ownership.
phase-plan.scope: In scope: first-party lead capture, scheduling intake, customer support intake, call and quote intake handoff, customer portal estimates/invoices/schedule/account surfaces, recurring service-plan defaults, internal CRM planning, Stripe billing direction, Resend lifecycle direction, and quote-to-opportunity planning inside Payload. Out of scope: customer-visible quote editing, finalized accounting sync, and full sequence automation implementation unless explicitly approved later.

file.description: Public-site conversion flow and content checklist.
System-of-record mapping for the in-house CRM, Stripe, Resend, and Payload jobs.
Product direction, data model, design rules, billing split, and automation roadmap for the in-house CRM.
Quote workflow and internal opportunity handoff planning.
Contact/support request categories and submission mapping.
Customer portal schedule page for appointments, recurring plans, and schedule-change requests.
Customer-facing invoice records used by the portal.
Scheduled or requested customer jobs used by the portal and ops calendar.
Recurring maintenance-plan records and calculated subscription totals.
Payload quote records that remain the estimate source for the customer portal and future internal opportunity pipeline.
Quote-side lifecycle hook used while external CRM sync is retired and internal opportunity flow is defined.
Current CRM abstraction boundary; now used to keep lead capture internal while the first-party CRM model replaces external providers.
Draftable recurring-plan defaults for the customer portal and service-plan records.

task.description: Keep every public lead captured in Payload with clear internal follow-up metadata, and move quote/contact/schedule/support capture onto a shared first-party form system.

Make scheduling part of the in-house customer lifecycle with a first-party form flow, shared validators/helpers, customer portal schedule requests/reschedules, recurring-plan cadence, and Payload-managed follow-up instead of an embedded vendor form.
Use Payload collections/globals for customer portal estimates, invoices, recurring plans, service appointments, and self-serve account/contact updates.
Retire external quote-to-deal sync and replace it with internal opportunity planning so follow-up and relationship management happen inside Payload.
Replace the old provider-toggle/day-board assumptions with internal queue, appointment, and opportunity reads so `/ops` uses Payload-native data sources only.

questions.question: Payload quotes remain the source of truth. The next step is an internal opportunity/pipeline model rather than syncing into a third-party CRM.
How should new leads, opportunities, and customer tasks be assigned to an owner inside the first-party CRM workspace?
No longer relevant. Third-party CRM providers are being removed from the active product direction.
